title: Make Your First $6,000/Month on YouTube (No Monetization Needed)

transcript.text: method is the easiest way to make money I've ever seen on the internet in 20 years plus. First month, $700 net profit. And now I'm just finishing my eighth month and I'm at $6,000 a month recurring revenue. So if I did nothing for the next 30 days, I guarantee I would make at least another 6,000. >> That is Reezy Resells. He makes $6,000 a month creating content. And no, this $6,000 a month is not from YouTube AdSense. He's been on YouTube since 2004. and now he's teaching people to do what he's been doing, making product reviews on Amazon that earn him enough money to where he could quit his full-time job. A lot of people could with $6,000 a month. But the more I dug in, the more I realized the overall potential ceiling that creators might have at their disposal here. It's massive. And literally any YouTube channel in any niche can try this. All you need is a cell phone camera. You don't even need to be monetized. And by the end of this video, I think you'll see just how much money you're potentially leaving on the table when you could be using skills that you've already built as a YouTube creator to make a whole lot more. Let's first talk about why this pays in general because it actually is rooted in something that you, as a creator on YouTube, might be pretty familiar with. Different niches pay different CPMs. A finance channel is going to make a lot more money than a gaming channel. And the reason, as you may already know, is that advertisers compete with one another as hard as they can to reach the best audience possible who will spend the most amount of money. The more valuable the buyer, the more they'll pay to get in front of them. Reezy's channel happens to be in the make money space on YouTube. So, here's what he had to say about CPMs. >> A $20 CPM is low for me. A $50 CPM is low for me. I've had over $200 CPMs before. >> For context, most creators would kill for a CPM of $15. And Reezy's over here with 200. >> I just accidentally fell into the highest paying niche possible. >> And the word accidentally is doing some heavy lifting here. What Reezy did, whether he knew it or not, was build an audience that had buying intent. These are people looking to spend money actively right now. And once you have that audience, or you make content that goes in front of them that has that intent, your views begin to snowball, as does the money that you're making from those videos. That same logic is how you get to $6,000 a month and counting. Which brings us to what Reezy is actually doing. On Amazon, just about every product page is going to have a video carousel, and you've probably scrolled past it hundreds of times without even thinking about it. Those videos are made by regular people, creators, through something called the Amazon Influencer Program. When someone watches one of those videos and then proceeds to buy the product, the creator who made that video gets a commission. Before we go any further, though, how do you actually get into this program in the first place? Well, you need a few things. A public account on YouTube, Instagram, Tik Tok, or Facebook. And one thing worth noting, if you're going through Instagram or Facebook, it can't just be a standard profile. It has to be a business or creator account. And that's just so Amazon gets access to the engagement data and other metrics that they're going to want to see. And you can only get those by having those types of profiles. So that's just one extra step. But beyond that, there is actually no follower minimum that you need. Amazon is weighing everything based on engagement. So if you already have a YouTube channel with any kind of active audience, you might be closer to getting into this program than you think. But here's what makes this different from Tik Tok shop or other affiliate programs you've probably heard about in the past. >> On Tik Tok, you have to go viral and then people see it and maybe buy it. But on Amazon, we're putting our videos on product pages that are essentially already viral because they have existing organic traffic. And that to me is like, oh, such a relief. I don't have to be creative. I don't have to do like a viral hook. >> The traffic is already there. You're not building an audience. You're walking into an existing one. One where the marketing was funded by someone else. You didn't have to do any of that work. And the reason this works so well specifically for creators is because you have something that Amazon can't manufacture. And that is the ability to tell a story. >> And so much of the knowledge I learned from being an Amazon seller and a content creator, it goes into this perfectly. >> The videos are 60 to 90 seconds, mostly one take. And the best part is that Reezy doesn't even have to buy the products. >> In July, I start sending 100 messages a day and products start showing up at my house. No less than 10 a day. Like 10 to 30 a day. But why do this? Why spend your valuable time negotiating for free products you can review on Amazon when you could be making more YouTube content? Well, the truth is most full-time creators all have something in common, and it's that YouTube AdSense is not their only source of income. In the case of Reezy and the other creators learning from him, they are taking all the skills that YouTube is helping them build like editing and storytelling and script writing and presenting and using them somewhere else. And in this instance, that place happens to be Amazon. But there's a bit more to what Reez is actually doing. Reezy operates in a space full of people trying to sell you on a dream. Make passive income from home, get rich quick, buy my course. >> There's a lot of people in the make money space, but a lot of those people, people like me, would never believe them because I would turn on your video, you're wearing a suit, you know what I mean? Your house looks like you have 10 maids, you have two Lamborghinis. It just doesn't seem real to me. Like maybe it is real. it just doesn't connect with me because I come from literally nothing. >> He knows exactly how his viewers feel when they see that content and why they leave. Which is why what he's built is kind of the opposite on purpose. By the way, that's another skill you're learning as a creator. Understanding your audience inside and out. >> I'm not the guy that's like, "Hey, you could get a Lambo." I'm the guy that's like, "Hey, I know you probably don't like your job. Here's another way you could make money in less time and then have more free time to control your life." So, I'm not saying like, "Here's how you get the Lambo. Here's how you become a millionaire." I'm saying here's how you own your time and once you own your time, the upside is unlimited. >> And that same principle carries directly into his product videos because Amazon's pages already have a brand video, one with CGI, professional production, AI, overpromising. But then there's Reezy. >> The reason people watch these videos is because the brand's video is like Yeti cup, you know? It's not even a real video. It's a CGI thing. It's way too perfect. And the other video is like a a real dude who has this cup and he has four stickers on it. He's clearly had it for a while. You know, like if you go too salesy, you're hurting yourself. Being a real person on camera, talking like you would talk to a friend and not performing for a crowd. These are things YouTube creators and content creators on all platforms are just doing. And that skill is building every time they hit the record button. But to take this a step further, there are even more YouTube parallels than you might think when it comes to reviewing products on Amazon. For example, we're always telling YouTubers to pick one niche and become the expert on that one thing. If your channel specializes in one thing, like mechanical keyboards, ultra light backpacking, or repairing vintage cameras, you're going to build a really strong audience avatar, and YouTube will know exactly who to show that content to. Well, that same thing is true on Amazon, and Reezy's starting point is actually kind of perfect for creators. Obviously, I'm a creator, so some of the first stuff I reviewed is all the stuff I'm looking at, like my Elgato Stream Deck or my Blackmagic or my Roadcaster or this SM7B, and I do make sales off those items. He started with the gear all over his desk, and it kind of makes sense, right? This is one of the things you specialize in. You're using this stuff every single day, and you can speak to it genuinely. But then he said something that really opened my mind up quite a bit because yeah, there's an opportunity to review things that everybody has. But there's an even larger opportunity to review things that not everybody has. You know, niche things. But I know people that are like, "Oh, I do in-home care for my mother and she has all these elderly products that are expensive things I never heard of. Like a a motorized recliner that like comes down lower for elderly people to get into easily and then takes them back. That's expensive. a chairlift that goes upstairs and bends the corner. That's expensive. And not only is it expensive, people probably aren't sending them as samples and other people like me aren't even thinking about those products. We don't have access to it. >> Just think for a second about what that means for you specifically. Do you have any niche sports that you like to participate in or hobbies where you collect things? Could you review little boxes that hold Pokémon cards? If you do have anything like that outside of YouTube creation, then you have a whole space full of stuff that is kind of niche to a very passionate specific group of people, but not everybody. If so, then you have to deal with less competition and you get more trust from people because you and them are on the same wavelength. You are part of the same exact hobby. You get to share your genuine point of view and one that can't easily be faked and that is the niche advantage working once again, just not here on YouTube. Let's talk about the money itself, specifically why it compounds like it does for Reezy. >> First month, $700 net profit. Second month, I did $2,200. Third month, 3,100. Fourth month, 4,400. And now I'm just finishing my eighth month, and I'm at 6,000 a month recurring revenue. So, if I did nothing for the next 30 days, I guarantee I would make it at least another 6,000, if not more, because that 6,000 is my rolling 30. And some of those videos have only been up for a week or two. They haven't even lived for 30 days. On top of that, brands will pay you to make videos, too. So, if I request, hey, I'd love to review this product. I drink coffee all the time. It'll be a knock out of the ballpark. So, they send me this Yeti cup. And then next week, they're like, "Oh, we loved your video. Can you do these products, too?" Now, I say, "Sure, I charge $50 a product, right? We might settle on 30, but to get paid 30 to make this video that took me 90 seconds. >> Every video he posts has the potential to keep earning, not because they go viral, but because that product is still relevant. People are still looking for it, and they're still watching that review every single day. >> It's it's so easy. Anybody could do it. Like even if you don't you don't have to be on camera. You can just show your hands. You could use AI to write scripts and because you're talking about products from Amazon, all the data is there already. People are like, "Well, you know, you got to really do this and do that." Like you don't have to make a cake in a cake pan to make a video about a cake pan. I might just like like try and flex it and punch it really hard and be like look how sturdy this thing is. You know, obviously if I've used it and I can then say, hey, had this for 3 months. I've made several cakes. There's no residue in it. It's still like that's a different kind of value and that's good too. I'll make that video later down the line too. But it's readily available opportunity for I was just looking at my my uh ATM Mini Blackmagic. I sold two of those yesterday and they're like 550 bucks. So the commission on that is was 3% so it's like over $15 for each unit, you know. So it's the way I tell people is like what if your blender could make you $50 a month, you know? Would that be cool? >> Yeah. Your blender, the thing just sitting on your counter ready to be used every single day. But that's not all you can do either. >> And then review everything you own in your house starting with the things that are most expensive or you know are most popular. Everybody in America has probably 500, if not 5,000 products in their home. I mean, every spice in your cabinet, every book you own, every cream, every brush, every anything. And here's where it gets even more interesting for creators specifically. You can take that same exact video and post it on a brand new YouTube channel where you post all of your reviews and attach an affiliate link in the description of that video. Again, the people who are looking for this specific product, have typed into the search bar, I want this thing, and there you are. Their buying intent is probably pretty high, and your competition depending on the item, is probably pretty low. And the coolest part about all of this is that your commission earning potential doesn't stop at reviewing just one product. If they happen to go Amazon and buy the product you had, or even didn't even do that, but they go, "Oh, I got to buy groceries or I got to buy this other thing. Heck, I had to buy a Sony camera." You get percentage of that even though you didn't make a video about that. if they go to Amazon and buy other things while they're there. And I think it's I don't think it's 24 hours. Yeah. >> So, if someone clicks your affiliate link to buy a cake pan, and they also continue shopping and throw a TV in the cart, now you're getting a commission on a television set. Here's the part of Reezy's story I keep coming back to. He's not telling you you're going to quit your job tomorrow, and he's not trying to say that you're going to be driving a Lamborghini. >> I really believe this is the best opportunity, and it can change anyone's life who pursues it seriously for like 90 days straight. You could quit your job. You could have more time with your kids. You could travel more. You could start saving more. You could pay off your debt. You could start a a second business, a passion business, a passion channel. You suddenly have your life back and can do what you want, not just what you have to do. >> So, his first instruction is to walk around your house, pick something up, and point a camera at it, and talk about it, which is stuff you're probably already used to doing if you're on YouTube. Reezy's links are in the description, and those Amazon product reviews you're about to make, yeah, you probably will want to put those on YouTube. And if you do, then you're probably going to want to understand how the algorithm actually works and surfaces content, which is what this video is for.
